content: Identify X, Y and Z formed in the reaction:

Which one of the following is the product $(\mathrm{Z})$ formed at the end of the given reaction:

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Propan-1-ol }+\mathrm{SOCl}_2 \rightarrow[\mathrm{X}] \\ & {[\mathrm{X}]+\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Y}]} \\ & {[\mathrm{Y}]+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 / \mathrm{HgSO}_4, 330 \mathrm{~K} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Z}]} \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
Let’s follow the three steps:

$$\text{CH}_3CH_2CH_2OH + \text{SOCl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_3CH_2CH_2Cl\quad(X)$$

(1-Propanol → propyl chloride)

$$\text{CH}_3CH_2CH_2Cl + \text{HC}\equiv\text{C–Na} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HC}\equiv\text{C–CH}_2CH_2CH_3\quad(Y)$$

(SN2 gives 1-pentyne)

Acid-catalyzed hydration (Markovnikov) with HgSO₄:

– Enol formation:

$$\text{HC}\equiv\text{C–(CH}_2)_2CH_3 + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; CH_2=C(OH)(CH_2)_2CH_3$$

– Tautomerization to the ketone:

$$CH_2=C(OH)(CH_2)_2CH_3 \;\longrightarrow\; CH_3COCH_2CH_2CH_3$$

That final product, CH₃COCH₂CH₂CH₃, is pentan-2-one.

Answer: Option D.

content: Choose the incorrect statement.

options.content:
The increasing order of boiling points of 5 compounds having comparable molar masses is as follows:
Methoxyethane $<$ Propanone $<$ Propanal $<$ Propan-1-ol
During nucleophilic addition of HCN to RCHO , the hybridisation changes from $\mathrm{sp}^2$ to $\mathrm{sp}^3$ and a tetrahedral intermediate is formed.
Acetone undergoes Wolff Kishner reaction to yield Propane.
The order of reactivity towards Nucleophilic attack is Methanal $>$ Di-tert.butyl ketone $>$ Benzaldehyde $>$ Acetophenone

explanation:
The two statements that are actually incorrect are A and D:

Option A: “Methoxyethane < Propanone < Propanal < Propan-1-ol”

– Propanone (acetone) boils at 56 °C, whereas propanal boils at 49 °C.

– The correct increasing b.p. order is

• Methoxyethane (–24 °C)

• Propanal (49 °C)

• Propanone (56 °C)

• Propan-1-ol (97 °C)

Option D: “Reactivity toward nucleophiles: Methanal > Di-tert-butyl ketone > Benzaldehyde > Acetophenone”

– Steric hindrance and electron-donation by the two tert-butyl groups make di-tert-butyl ketone the least reactive.

– Benzaldehyde is more reactive than acetophenone (only one electron-donating CH₃ vs. conjugated phenyl) and far more reactive than a heavily hindered aliphatic ketone.

– The correct electrophilic order is roughly

$$\text{Formaldehyde} > \text{Benzaldehyde} > \text{Acetophenone} > \text{Di-tert-butyl ketone}\,. $$

Statements B and C are both correct.

content:
An organic compound " A " reacts with $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Hg}$ / Conc. HCl to form p-Xylene. It reduces Tollen's reagent and on oxidation with $\mathrm{KMnO}_4 / \mathrm{H}^{+}$it yields 1, 4-benzenedicarboxylic acid.
Identify compound A.
explanation:
The only structure that fits all three tests is p-methylbenzaldehyde (4-methylbenzaldehyde).

• Tollen’s test positive ⇒ A must have an –CHO group (rules out phenols and ketones).

• Clemmensen (Zn/Hg, HCl) reduces –CHO to –CH₃ ⇒ p-methylbenzaldehyde becomes 1,4-dimethylbenzene (p-xylene).

• KMnO₄/H⁺ oxidizes both the –CHO and the –CH₃ to –COOH ⇒ gives 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (terephthalic acid).

Answer: Option A, p-methylbenzaldehyde.

content: Which one of the following reactions does not give the correct combination of reactants and the major products formed in the reaction?

C. Benzaldehyde

D. 2-Phenyl ethanol
What is/are the product/s formed when Benzaldehyde and Ethanal react in presence of dil. $$\mathrm{NaOH}$$ followed by heating the intermediate product formed?
explanation:
Benzaldehyde ($$\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO}$$) and Ethanal ($$\mathrm{CH_3CHO}$$) react in the presence of dilute sodium hydroxide ($$\mathrm{NaOH}$$) through a reaction known as the Aldol Condensation. This reaction specifically leads to the formation of an α,β-unsaturated aldehyde.

The mechanism involves the following steps:

1. Addition Step: Ethanal, in the presence of $$\mathrm{NaOH}$$, undergoes nucleophilic addition where the enolate ion formed from Ethanal attacks the carbonyl carbon of Benzaldehyde.

2. Formation of Aldol: This results in the formation of 3-hydroxy-3-phenylpropanal as the β-hydroxy aldehyde intermediate.

3. Dehydration Step: Heating this intermediate leads to the loss of a water molecule, resulting in the formation of an α,β-unsaturated aldehyde:

The balanced chemical equation for the overall reaction can be represented as:

$$\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO + CH_3CHO \xrightarrow{NaOH} C_6H_5-CH=CH-CHO + H_2O}$$

The product formed in this reaction is 3-Phenylprop-2-enal (commonly known as cinnamaldehyde).

Therefore, the option B (3-Phenylprop-2-enal & But-2-enal) is the correct answer. Actually, there is no But-2-enal formed; only cinnamaldehyde is produced.

Hence, the corrected answer is:

Correct Answer: Option B - 3-Phenylprop-2-enal

content:
Which one of the following is the correct order of reagents to be used to convent [A] to [X] ?

$$ \text { Reagents: } \mathrm{PCC}, \mathrm{PCl}_5, \mathrm{LiAlH}_4, \frac{\mathrm{KCN}}{\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}} $$
Predict the final major product 'S' of the following reaction
Which of the following compounds undergo Aldol condensation followed by dehydration to give 3-phenylprop-2-en-1-al?
explanation:
We want to obtain 3‑phenylprop‑2‑en‑1‑al, which has the structure

$$\ce{Ph-CH=CH-CHO}$$

This compound is more commonly known as cinnamaldehyde. An aldol condensation that forms such an α,β‑unsaturated aldehyde requires that:

• One of the reactants has α‑hydrogens (so it can form an enolate ion), and

• The other reactant is non–enolizable (no α‑hydrogens) so it serves as the electrophilic carbonyl partner.

Let’s review each option:

• Option A (Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone):

  – Benzaldehyde has no α‑hydrogens, which is good for the electrophile.

  – Acetophenone does have α‑hydrogens and can form an enolate.

  – However, the condensation between benzaldehyde and acetophenone would yield an α,β‑unsaturated ketone (a chalcone derivative containing two phenyl rings), not an aldehyde.

• Option B (Two molecules of Benzaldehyde):

  – Benzaldehyde does not have any α‑hydrogens.

  – Without α‑hydrogens, it cannot form an enolate, so an aldol condensation cannot take place.

• Option C (Benzaldehyde and Acetone):

  – Acetone can form an enolate (it has α‑hydrogens) and benzaldehyde can act as the electrophile.

  – However, the resulting product after dehydration would be an α,β‑unsaturated ketone, not the desired α,β‑unsaturated aldehyde.

• Option D (Benzaldehyde and Ethanal):

  – Ethanal (acetaldehyde) has α‑hydrogens and can form an enolate.

  – Benzaldehyde, which lacks α‑hydrogens, serves as the electrophile.

  – The nucleophilic enolate from ethanal attacks benzaldehyde to give a β‑hydroxyaldehyde intermediate, which then undergoes dehydration to yield

$$\ce{Ph-CH=CH-CHO},$$

which is precisely 3‑phenylprop‑2‑en‑1‑al (cinnamaldehyde).

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: Benzaldehyde and Ethanal.

content: Acetophenone when reacted with a base, $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{ONa}$$, yields a stable compound which has the structure
explanation: Aldehydes or ketones with $$\alpha-\mathrm{H}$$ atom, in presence of dilute base, undergoes aldol condensation to give $$\beta$$-hydroxy aldehyde or ketone. On heating, aldol eliminate water molecule to form $$\alpha, \beta$$-unsaturated compounds.

content: Which of the following is incorrect?

options.content: Primary alcohols are very easily oxidised to aldehydes, which are oxidised to acids with same number of C-atoms.
Secondary alcohols are very easily oxidised to ketones, which are oxidised to acids with same number of C-atoms.
Secondary alcohols are easily oxidised to ketones, which are oxidised to acids with lesser number of $$\mathrm{C}$$-atoms.
Secondary and tertiary alcohols on oxidation form acids with lesser number of C-atoms.

explanation:
Primary alcohols are easily oxidised to aldehydes and then to acid, both containing the same number of carbon atoms, while secondary alcohols are easily oxidised to ketones with same number of carbon atoms, but ketone oxidised to carboxylic acid containing lesser number of carbon atoms than original alcohol.

Thus all alcohols on oxidation finally give acids but acids obtained from $$2^{\circ}$$ and $$3^{\circ}$$ alcohols contain less C-atom.

content: Which one of the following compounds would not undergo Aldol condensation?

From among the following, identify the compound which forms two moles of a ketone on ozonolysis.

[A] 2,3-Dimethylbutane.

[B] 3-Methyl-1-pentene.

[C] 2,3-Dimethyl-2-butene.

[D] 2-Methyl-2-pentane.
explanation:
To determine which compound forms two moles of a ketone on ozonolysis, we need to understand the process of ozonolysis and the structures of the compounds given. Ozonolysis is a reaction that breaks alkene double bonds using ozone, forming two carbonyl compounds (aldehydes or ketones) from the alkene.

[A] 2,3-Dimethylbutane is an alkane (no double bonds), so it will not undergo ozonolysis. Therefore, it cannot form ketones through this process.

[B] 3-Methyl-1-pentene has a double bond between the carbon atoms in the 1st and 2nd positions. However, ozonolysis of this compound will not yield two moles of a ketone; it will likely produce a mixture of a ketone and an aldehyde, due to the nature of the substituents around the double bond.

[C] 2,3-Dimethyl-2-butene is a more promising candidate because it has a double bond at the 2nd position, and with methyl groups on both carbons involved in the double bond, ozonolysis will indeed yield two moles of the same ketone due to the symmetry of the molecule.

[D] 2-Methyl-2-pentane is an alkane (no double bonds), similar to option A, and also cannot undergo ozonolysis to produce ketones.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C [2,3-Dimethyl-2-butene], as it is the only compound listed that, when subjected to ozonolysis, will form two moles of a ketone, specifically acetone in this case. The reaction proceeds by cleaving the double bond and forming two carbonyl groups, one on each of the former alkene carbon atoms, because of the symmetrically substituted double bond.

What would be the products obtained when a mixture of p-Methoxy benzaldehyde and Methanal are heated with $$50 \%$$ concentrated Caustic soda solution?

options.content: p- Methoxy sodium benzoate and sodium acetate.
p-Methoxy benzyl alcohol and sodium formate.
p- Methoxy benzyl alcohol and methanol.
p- Methoxy sodium benzoate and sodium formate

explanation:
The reaction described involves a mixture of p-methoxy benzaldehyde (para-methoxybenzaldehyde) and methanal (formaldehyde) with concentrated caustic soda (sodium hydroxide, NaOH) solution. In this scenario, the reaction is a crossed Cannizzaro reaction. The Cannizzaro reaction is a redox reaction where an aldehyde is transformed into an alcohol and a carboxylic acid (or its salt) when treated with a strong base.

In the crossed Cannizzaro reaction, two different aldehydes that lack alpha-hydrogens (like methanal and p-methoxy benzaldehyde) react. The more electrophilic aldehyde (typically the one more capable of stabilizing negative charge) will tend to form the carboxylate, while the other aldehyde gets reduced to the corresponding alcohol.

p-Methoxy benzaldehyde has the ability to stabilize negative charge through resonance due to the electron-donating methoxy group which makes the aldehyde carbon less electrophilic compared to methanal. This allows methanal to preferentially form the carboxylate salt under the reaction conditions. Therefore, in the presence of a strong base like NaOH:

  • Methanal gets oxidized to form sodium formate ($$\text{HCOO}^- \text{Na}^+$$).
  • p-Methoxy benzaldehyde gets reduced to form p-methoxy benzyl alcohol ($$\text{CH}_3\text{O-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$).

Therefore, the correct product of this reaction would be p-Methoxy benzyl alcohol and sodium formate. Looking at the given options, the correct answer is:

Option B: p-Methoxy benzyl alcohol and sodium formate.
